entities.name: All Souls
entities.raw_quote: Wherever the Roman Catholic Church exists, solemn Mass for All Souls is said on the 2nd November...

entities.name: All Saints

attributes.status: retained despite rejection of All Souls

entities.raw_quote: ...the Church of England...clings devoutly to All Saints.

entities.name: Aya Marca

attributes.description: Peruvian festival of the dead with tears, lugubrious songs, plaintive music, tomb visits, food/drink offerings

entities.raw_quote: "The month...is called 'Aya Marca,' from 'Aya', a 'corpse', and 'Marca', 'carrying in arms', because they celebrated the solemn festival of the dead with tears, lugubrious songs and plaintive music...

entities.name: Dii Manes
entities.raw_quote: In Rome the festival of the dead, or 'Feralia,' called 'Dii Manes,' or 'the day of the spirits of the dead'...

entities.name: Lemuria

attributes.date: May 11th (17th day of second month in old Latin calendar)
attributes.description: Festival of spirits believed to be souls of deceased friends

entities.raw_quote: "In more ancient times, the 'festival of the spirits,' believed to be the souls of deceased friends, was called 'Lemuria'...

entities.name: Anthesteria

attributes.cultural link: connected to same event as Egyptian mourning for Osiris

entities.raw_quote: A feast called the 'Anthesteria' was also celebrated at Athens on February llth-13th, in honour of Bacchus, who was identical with the Egyptian Osiris...

entities.name: Osiris

attributes.myth: shut up in the deep for one year like Noah, resurrection celebrated

entities.raw_quote: ...the seventeenth day of their second month commenced their solemn mourning for Osiris, the Lord of Tombs, who was fabled to have been 'shut up in the deep for one year like Noah...'

entities.name: Pleiades

attributes.astronomical role: marker for festivals of dead and agriculture across multiple cultures

entities.raw_quote: "Mr Haliburton has some interesting arguments to prove that the festival in many nations was fixed by the first rising of the Pleiades above the horizon."

entities.name: Matarii i nia

attributes.function: festival of the dead and first-fruits, marks start of year

entities.raw_quote: "This festival of the dead and of the first-fruits is evidently that referred to by Ellis...called by them 'Matarii i nia,' or 'The Pleiades above'...

entities.name: Deluge

attributes.significance: foundation for global mourning festivals and ancestor veneration

entities.raw_quote: "...one or other of the two great events in the history of the Deluge...were observed throughout the ancient world...intimately connected with...that worship of the dead which...was the central principle of the ancient idolatry."

entities.name: Babylon

attributes.influence: source of religion for Egypt, Greece, Rome, etc.

entities.raw_quote: "It would appear probable that...the very unity of the religious system...emanated from Babylon...

entities.name: Chaldeans

attributes.language: Sumerian/Accadian sacred language used for incantations

entities.raw_quote: "...the Chaldees to the Egyptians and thence to the Greeks...used for their incantations and magical sorceries by the Assyrians...

entities.name: Etruscans
entities.raw_quote: "Colonel Conder...shows that the Etruscan language was remarkably similar to the ancient Chaldean or Accadian."

entities.name: Saturn
entities.raw_quote: "...two cities...called Saturnia and Janicula...Saturn was certainly of Chaldean origin."

entities.name: Cadmus

attributes.contributions: introduced Phoenician/Egyptian gods, letters to Greeks; founded Thebes (after Egyptian city)

entities.raw_quote: "Much of the religion of Greece was introduced by Cadmus the 'Phoenician', who...taught the Greeks the worship of Phoenician and Egyptian gods...

entities.name: Hesiod

attributes.relevance: cited as evidence that gods were deified men

entities.raw_quote: "Hesiod...says that 'the gods were the souls of men who were afterwards worshipped by their posterity, on account of their extraordinary virtues.'"

relationships.type: distinct titles of same divine incarnation

summary: This chunk explores cross-cultural parallels in death rituals and ancestor veneration, emphasizing the near-universal timing of such festivals around November or early spring, often linked to Pleiades visibility. It traces shared motifs—including mourning for Osiris/Adonis, Roman Feralia/Lemuria, Greek Anthesteria—to a common primordial origin, which the text attributes to Babylon and the Deluge tradition. The chapter argues that ancient deities originated as deified ancestors whose worship became entangled with astral phenomena and cosmological cycles.
key_passages: "Wherever the Roman Catholic Church exists, solemn Mass for All Souls is said on the 2nd November..."
"The Egyptians began their year at the same time as the Jews, and on the seventeenth day of their second month commenced their solemn mourning for Osiris, the Lord of Tombs..."
"In Rome the festival of the dead, or 'Feralia,' called 'Dii Manes,' or 'the day of the spirits of the dead'...
"The festival of the dead was held on the 28th November [MS: likely typo for 1st Nov], and was regulated by the Pleiades..."
"Hesiod...says that 'the gods were the souls of men who were afterwards worshipped by their posterity, on account of their extraordinary virtues.'"
